data.title: Chip stocks with the best momentum going into Nvidia earnings
data.description:
The semiconductor market is positioned to have another "transformative year" in 2025, believes exchange-traded fund provider VanEck.

Industry leaders such as Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), TSMC (TSM), and Marvell (MRVL) have benefitted from the ever-increasing demand for semiconductors fueled by advancements in artificial intelligence.

The world's most valuable chipmaker, Nvidia (NVDA), is expected to post strong results and guidance when it reports quarterly results next week, despite the GB200 NVL constraints it has faced.

Nvidia (NVDA) is slated to report its quarterly results after the close of trading on February 26. Analysts expect the company to earn $0.61 per share on $26.044B in revenue.

Here are the top 12 semiconductor stocks with the highest momentum according to the Seeking Alpha Quant Rating system. The Quant system is driven by powerful computer processing and SA's special 'Quantamental' analysis. It awards grades to stocks based on collective value, growth, profitability, earnings per share revisions, and price momentum metrics.

No. 1: Credo Technology Group (CRDO), momentum - A+

No. 2: Himax Technologies (HIMX), momentum - A+

No. 3: Rigetti Computing (RGTI), momentum - A+

No. 4: Ambarella (AMBA), momentum - A

No. 5: Broadcom (AVGO), momentum - A

No. 6: Kopin (KOPN), momentum - A

No. 7: POET Technologies (POET), momentum - A

No. 8: ACM Research (ACMR), momentum - A-

No. 9: Atomera (ATOM), momentum - A-

No. 10: Marvell Technology (MRVL), momentum - A-

No. 11: Nova (NVMI), momentum - A-

No. 12: Silicon Laboratories, momentum - A-

Semiconductor ETFs: (SMH), (SOXX), (SOXL), (FTXL), (XSD), (USD), (PSI), and (NYSEARCA:SEMI)

data.title: AppLovin, CRH are among ‘shared favorites’ for fund managers: Goldman Sachs
data.description:
AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP), CRH (NYSE:CRH), Mastercard (NYSE:MA), Spotify (NYSE:SPOT) and Visa (NYSE:V) at the end of the fourth quarter were “shared favorites” stocks among hedge funds and mutual funds, according to strategists at financial-services firm Goldman Sachs. The firm also said hedge funds appear to be more selective than mutual funds with their positions in financial-industry stocks.

“The stocks that are both hedge fund and mutual fund favorites have a track record of outperformance but at the expense of higher volatility,” David Kostin, strategist at Goldman Sachs, said in a February 21 report. “Since 2013, shared favorites have delivered an annual return of 16% with a standard deviation of 21%.”

AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP), an app marketing platform, was the only addition to the list of shared favorites during the fourth quarter of 2024, according to Goldman Sachs. The stock was up 39% year-to-date through the market close Friday, and surged more than sevenfold in the prior 12 months.

As a group, the shared favorites are up 14% so far this year, beating the Standard & Poor’s 500 (SP500) stock index by 10 percentage points. However, the stocks are richly priced, trading at a median price-to-earnings ratio of 35 times, compared with the S&P 500’s 19 times.

Eliminated from the list of shared favorites during the fourth quarter were Fiserv (NYSE:FI), Progressive (NYSE:PGR) and Vertiv Holdings (NYSE:VRT), according to Goldman Sachs.

Differing views on financials

Mutual funds on average increased their exposure to stocks in the financial-services industry, while hedge funds “backed away” during the fourth quarter, Goldman Sachs said.

The difference is notable because going into Election Day, both kinds of funds had increased their exposure to financials, viewing the industry as a “beneficiary of a pro-growth and deregulatory policy agency agenda.”

However, by the end of the quarter, mutual funds increased their overweight position in financials by 36 basis points to 223 basis points. Conversely, hedge funds swung from 135 basis points overweight in the third quarter to 41 basis points underweight in the fourth quarter for “the largest cut across sectors.”

Hedge funds cut exposure to diversified banks such as Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC), U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) and Bank of America (NYSE:BAC), diversified financial services such as Apollo Global Management (NYSE:APO) and transaction and payment processing services such as PayPal Holdings (NASDAQ:PYPL).

Hedge funds added to regional banks and brokers such as Robinhood Markets (NASDAQ:HOOD), which Goldman Sachs said was a “rising star” in the fourth quarter.

Meanwhile, mutual funds broadly increased their exposure to financials, especially in banks, capital markets and insurance. More specifically, Goldman Sachs found six “popular positions” where large-cap mutual funds added exposure in the fourth quarter: Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC), Mastercard (NYSE:MA), Bank of America (NYSE:BAC), Charles Schwab (NYSE:SCHW), U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) and Citigroup (NYSE:C).

data.title: Top global stories this week: HSBC, Alibaba, Airbus among notable names
data.description:
Globally, all major stock indexes ticked downwards on a weekly basis, following negative economic news and mixed earnings performances throughout the week. Declines were also driven by tariff concerns.

S&P 500 notched up a record high on Wednesday but ended the week lower over gloomy economic updates. Walmart's weaker-than-expected forecast has raised concerns about consumer health, particularly after a soft retail sales report the previous week.

U.S. business activity growth nearly stalled in February, as a decline in services output countered stronger manufacturing growth. The initial jobless claims rose more than consensus in past week, while continuing claims came in line.

The world’s largest economy ended the week. Nasdaq 100 (COMP:IND) fell 0.4%, S&P 500 index SP500 was down 0.2% and Dow Jones (DJI) dropped 0.9% in the week.

Across the Atlantic, the European equities (STOXX) notched a fresh record high on Tuesday, but ended the week lower. Investors assessed a slew of mixed corporate earnings from industry giants, while lower PMI data from the region reflected weakness in the economy.

London (UKX) equities also ended the week nearly 1% lower. UK inflation rose more than expected to 3% in January, the highest in 10 months, and may continue to climb. This challenges the Bank of England's belief that inflation will fall in the long term. Traders now expect only modest rate cuts due to weak economic growth and ongoing inflation.

France (CAC:IND) and Germany (DAX:IND) ended the week 0.2%, and ~1% lower, respectively.

Major news from Europe included declines in earnings for natural resources and aerospace, strong profits in banking and electrical sectors, and share buybacks from financial firms.

Global natural resource company Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) posted a year-on-year decline in adjusted earnings in its preliminary annual results.

Aerospace manufacturer Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) reported an 8% year-on-year drop in adjusted operating profit, despite a 6% jump in revenue.

Europe’s largest lender HSBC (HSBC) reported an annual pre-tax profit of $32.31 billion on Wednesday and initiated a share buy-back of up to $2 billion.

Electrical equipment maker Schneider Electric (OTCPK:SBGSF) reported record annual sales and profits.

British bank Standard Chartered (OTCPK:SCBFF) posted a decline in Q4 net profit but announced a $1.5 billion share buyback.

In Asian markets, the People's Bank of China kept its key lending rates unchanged for the fourth consecutive month in February, meeting expectations. Chinese President Xi Jinping pledged to support the private sector. Meanwhile, US President Donald Trump suggested that a new trade deal with China was possible, signalling a more positive outlook for US-China relations, after a week of cautious trading over tariff concerns.

China (SHCOMP) grew 0.3% and Hong Kong (HSI) rose 0.7% while Japan (NKY:IND) fell 1.9% in the last week.

Japan's economy grew in the fourth quarter, exceeding expectations and showing an improvement from the previous quarter. Additionally, Japan reported a larger-than-anticipated trade deficit for January, with imports outpacing exports. Core inflation ticked higher in January, beating estimates.

Asian corporate news revolved around earnings, and acquisition interests.

A high-level Japanese group, including former Prime Minister Yoshihide Suga, is proposing that Tesla (TSLA) invest in Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) following the fallout of merger talks with Honda (HMC). Meanwhile, Honda is ready to revive merger talks with Nissan if the latter's CEO Makoto Uchida resigns.

Japanese system integration company Fuji Soft has been acquired by KKR (KKR), which purchased a 57.92% stake for $4.4B, winning a competitive takeover battle against Bain Capital (BCSF).

Japan's Mitsui & Co. (OTCPK:MITSY) announced the acquisition of 40% interest in the Rio Tinto-operated (RIO) Rhodes Ridge iron ore project in Western Australia for $5.34B.

Chinese e-commerce giant Alibaba (BABA) reported third-quarter revenues just above analysts' estimates and said it plans to invest more in e-commerce and AI.

Artificial intelligence startup DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) said it will be sharing five code repositories publicly starting next week, as it looks to progress with “full transparency.”

Video sharing and gaming platform Bilibili (BILI) reported a top-and-bottom line beat for its fourth quarter results, as strong growth in advertising and mobile games businesses helped the YouTube of China post its first-ever GAAP profit.

Internet and game services provider NetEase (NTES) missed the top and bottom lines in its fourth quarter earnings report, as growth declined in all its major reporting segments along with a marginal rise in its core gaming unit.

ETFs to track Europe: ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

ETFs to track Asia & Oceania: (NYSEARCA:EWJ), (NYSE:JEQ), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:DXJ), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:FXY), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSEARCA:KWEB)

data.title: Musk's justify-your-job mail sparks confusion, with some federal agencies asking employees to hold off replying
data.description:
Elon Musk's mandate to federal government agencies, asking them to describe their recent job achievements seems to have caught many off-guard.

Management across multiple agencies have informed staff that they are awaiting further instructions and have asked employees to hold off on replying, ABC News reported.

Musk, the head of the newly created Department of Government Efficiency, on Saturday said all federal employees will have to share what they've been working on in the last week or face dismissal.

Musk's announcement followed President Donald Trump's post on his own social media network, Truth Social, urging DOGE to get more aggressive in its efforts to trim the 2.3 million strong federal workforce.

Chaos and Confusion

Federal agencies have received an email from the Office of Personnel Management with the subject line "What did you do last week?," asking employees to summarize in five bullet points what they had accomplished at work last week. The email gives workers until 11:59 p.m. EST on Monday to respond.

Agencies that have asked employees to hold off on replying include the Federal Emergency Management Agency, the Internal Revenue Service, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and the Department of Energy, ABC News said.

The Pentagon has ordered civilian employees to wait for legal guidance before responding, ABC News added. The Federal Bureau of Investigation has also asked employees to "pause" any responses to the OPM mail.

Justice Department supervisors have directed employees to not reply to the Musk mail, Bloomberg reported, an order that DOJ officials worry will trigger ethical violations.

Top leadership at the State Department has asked employees to ignore Musk's productivity inquiry, NBC News reporter Yamiche Alcindor said in a post on X.

Some federal judiciary employees too received the OPM mail on Saturday, even though the court system is not part of the executive branch, Reuters reported.

Employees at the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau received the mail, Reuters added, even though most agency workers have been ordered to not perform any tasks since early this month.

It is unclear as of now what legal basis Musk has to fire federal workers if they fail to respond to his query, and what would happen to employees that cannot detail confidential work.

Union Vows to Fight Back

The American Federation of Government Employees or AFGE has promised to legally challenge "any unlawful terminations."

"Once again, Elon Musk and the Trump Administration have shown their utter disdain for federal employees and the critical services they provide to the American people," said AFGE national president Everett Kelley.

Since coming into existence on January 20 through an executive order signed by President Trump, Musk has promised that DOGE's cost-cutting effort would provide "maximum transparency" and that "all of our actions are fully public."

While DOGE claims the cost-cutting department has saved $55 billion in government spending so far, a Bloomberg report says the entity's official website only accounts for $16.6 billion of that.

data.title: Notable healthcare headlines for the week: UnitedHealth, Moderna, Sanofi in focus
data.description:
Wall Street losses accelerated on Friday, with investors closing out the week with gloomy economic updates and report of a government investigation into UnitedHealth (UNH) in hand.

However, the S&P 500 Health Care Index Sector (NYSEARCA:XLV) gained 1.87% during the week.

The top S&P500 healthcare gainers and losers for the last one week are as follows:

Top Gainers:

  • Baxter International (BAX) +10.84%
  • Moderna (MRNA) +10.49%
  • Charles River Laboratories International (CRL) +6.37%
  • Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA) +5.61%
  • West Pharmaceutical Services (WST) +5.57%

· Top Losers:

  • DaVita (DVA) -19.58%
  • UnidtedHealth (UNH) -12.11%
  • Align Technology (ALGN) -7.47%
  • Cooper Companies (COO) -5.20%
  • CVS Health (CVS) -4.26%

Here are some of the important healthcare stories from this week:

UnitedHealth falls after report on new DoJ probe

Shares of UnitedHealth Group (UNH) dropped over 7% on Friday after The Wall Street Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter, that the DoJ launched a civil fraud investigation related to how the company adds questionable diagnoses to its members’ medical records to benefit from extra payments to its Medicare Advantage plans.

Gilead upped on HIV franchise; Merck downgraded on Keytruda LoE at Deutsche Bank

Deutsche Bank upgraded Gilead Sciences (GILD) to Buy from Hold previously, citing the antiviral developer’s HIV treatment franchise. The upgrade coincided with the FDA’s decision to grant priority review for lenacapavir as a PrEP therapy for HIV prevention this week.

On the other hand, the brokerage firm downgraded Merck (MRK) to Hold from Buy, stating that the cancer drugmaker’s upcoming loss of exclusivity for its blockbuster therapy Keytruda. Analyst James Shin lowered its price target to $105 from $128 per share.

Medtronic slips after reaffirming full-year guidance:

Despite an opportunity to consolidate its lead in the market for pulse field ablation systems following a setback to rival J&J (JNJ), the MedTech giant Medtronic reaffirmed its full-year outlook, resulting in shared tumbling over 7% on Tuesday. However, Medtronic (MDT) said it continued to expect its FY25 organic revenue growth to reach 4.75% - 5% and its diluted non-GAAP earnings per share to stand at $5.44 - $5.50 in line with $5.45 per share in the consensus.

In the M&A news of the week, Sanofi (SNY) signed a share purchase agreement in relation to the sale of a 50% controlling stake in Opella, its consumer healthcare business, to private equity firm CD&R. Sanofi first entered into negotiations with CD&R for the potential sale in October 2024. Bloomberg, at the time, had reported the deal value to be around EUR 15B, or $16.4B.

Meanwhile, despite a downgrade from Barclays, Moderna jumped over 8% on Tuesday, mainly due to management’s comment on prioritizing 10 high-value programs with possible approval over the next three years which are geared toward non-COVID treatments. However, the investment bank downgraded the biotech to equal weight from overweight citing a lack of clinical catalysts, cutting its price target to $45 from $111.

Among other news that grabbed headlines this week includes a bipartisan group of U.S. state and territory attorneys general pressing the FDA to take prompt action against those threatening public health by marketing counterfeit forms of branded weight loss medications developed by Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY). Their concerns come at a time when the two leading obesity drugmakers struggle to maintain an adequate supply of their hugely popular GLP-1 meds.

data.title: Citi sees recovery in infrastructure demand with AI 'lumpiness' ahead of IT hardware earnings
data.description:
Several IT hardware companies are set to report quarterly results next week and Citi said it has seen a broader recovery in the demand for infrastructure, albeit with some “lumpiness” coming from artificial intelligence-related spending.

“From a fundamental perspective, overall infrastructure demand indicators (excluding AI) remain constructive with supply chain commentary (SNX, CDW) suggesting a broader recovery in infrastructure demand (with pent-up demand as mainstream workloads continue),” Citi analyst Asiya Merchant wrote in a note to clients.

“On AI, bullish expectations ahead are offset with near term lumpiness and margin pressures amidst chip transitions,” Merchant added. “PC demand is expected to recover in 2025 given Windows 10 [end of life] though remains lackluster near term with some pull-forward on order builds likely in 1Q amidst potential tariff impacts.”

Dell

Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL) is set to report on Feb. 27 and Merchant lowered her estimates “modestly” due to margin concerns related to AI servers, due mostly to supply constraints related to the processor transition.

“While [Infrastructure Solutions Group] margins are likely to be pressured near term, we believe AI server margins will improve ahead given broadening adoption which enables for more customization, and benefits from opex leverage as scale broadens,” Merchant wrote.

Merchant, who reiterated her Buy rating on Dell, said she expects the company to guide “conservatively” for fiscal 2026.

A consensus of analysts expect Dell to earn $2.51 per share on $24.59B in revenue for the coming quarter.

HP

HP (NYSE:HPQ) is also set to report on Feb. 27 and while the company's printer business has made market share gains and improved margins, there is “limited” upside from the PC business, Merchant, who reiterated her Neutral rating on HP, said.

A consensus of analysts expect HP to earn $0.74 per share on $13.35B in revenue.

Pure Storage

Pure Storage (NYSE:PSTG) is set to report Feb. 26 and Merchant reiterated her Buy rating, as the storage company has benefited from momentum in flash and storage-as-a-service in a “moderately improving spend environment.”

“We remain constructive as we believe [the] recently announced hyperscaler win is likely to translate to traction at other hyperscalers and merit a slightly higher multiple for our revised $80 TP, up from $75 prior,” Merchant wrote.

A consensus of analysts expect Pure Storage to earn $0.42 per share on $868.94M revenue.

NetApp

NetApp (NASDAQ:NTAP) is also set to report Feb. 27 and Merchant said the company remains “well positioned” to benefit from the longer-term storage modernization trends.

However, it faces tougher comparisons and increased competition from Dell and other vendors, and “meaningful” upside is unlikely until a broader uptick in enterprise storage spending occurs. Merchant maintained her Neutral rating.

A consensus of analysts expect NetApp to earn $1.91 per share on $1.69B revenue.

data.title: Earnings week ahead: NVDA, HPQ, CRM, ZM, HD, and more
data.description:
As February draws to a close, investors prepare for another packed week of earnings, with major companies across tech, retail, real estate, energy and entertainment set to report their latest results.

Leading the lineup, NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) is expected to command attention, as investors assess the continued impact of AI on its business. The tech sector will also feature Salesforce (NYSE:CRM), Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW), Zoom (NASDAQ:ZM), C3.ai (NYSE:AI), Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL) and HP Inc. (NYSE:HPQ), offering a glimpse into cloud computing, AI and enterprise software trends.

The retail and consumer sectors will be in focus as Home Depot (NYSE:HD), Lowe’s (NYSE:LOW), eBay (NASDAQ:EBAY) and Beyond Meat (NASDAQ:BYND) release their results, shedding light on consumer spending patterns.

The energy and industrial sectors will also be active, with reports from First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR), Expand Energy, Petróleo Brasileiro (NYSE:PBR), ONEOK (NYSE:OKE) and EOG Resources (NYSE:EOG). Investors will also be watching Cleveland-Cliffs (NYSE:CLF) for insights into the steel industry.

In the financial and real estate sector, earnings from Realty Income (NYSE:O), Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN), The Toronto-Dominion Bank (NYSE:TD), Medical Properties Trust (NYSE:MPW), and American Tower (NYSE:AMT) will be closely watched.

Riot Platforms (NASDAQ:RIOT) and MARA Holdings (NASDAQ:MARA) will provide key insights into the cryptocurrency mining sector. In media and entertainment, investors will be watching Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD), AMC Entertainment (NYSE:AMC), and Paramount Global (NASDAQ:PARA).

Other key earnings to watch include Li Auto (NASDAQ:LI), Lucid (NASDAQ:LCID), Stellantis (NYSE:STLA), Teladoc Health (NYSE:TDOC), Viatris (NASDAQ:VTRS), Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY), fuboTV (NYSE:FUBO) and Virgin Galactic (NYSE:SPCE).

Below is a rundown of major quarterly updates anticipated in the week of February 24 to February 28:

Monday, February 24

Zoom Video Communications

Zoom Video Communications (ZM) is scheduled to report its Q4 results after Monday’s close, with analysts anticipating a mixed performance.

SA author Kevin George sees potential for gains, noting that Zoom, down about 85% from its pandemic peak, has matured into a solid investment opportunity. He highlights strong net margins, a healthy cash balance, and undervaluation metrics as key positives.

However, SA author Steven Fiorillo remains neutral on the stock. Despite strong free cash flow and solid fundamentals, he points to stagnant revenue growth and rising R&D expenses as concerns. He warns that competition from Microsoft Teams and Google Workspace adds pressure, and without new products, Zoom may struggle to accelerate growth.

Overall, sell-side analysts remain cautious, assigning Zoom a consensus Hold rating. Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system recommends a Buy.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $1.35
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $1.19B
  • Earnings Insight: Zoom has beaten EPS and revenue estimates in 100% of the past 8 quarters.

Also reporting: Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O), Cleveland-Cliffs (NYSE:CLF), Riot Platforms (NASDAQ:RIOT), ONEOK (NYSE:OKE), Clean Energy Fuels (CLNE), Diamondback Energy (FANG), Coterra Energy (CTRA), LTC Properties (LTC), Domino's Pizza (DPZ) and more.

Tuesday, February 25

The Home Depot

Home Depot (HD) is set to announce its Q4 results before Tuesday’s market open, with analysts expecting 7.5% profit growth and a nearly 12% revenue increase.

Despite Wall Street analysts maintaining a bullish Buy rating, Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings system takes a more cautious Hold stance, flagging concerns about growth and valuation. Meanwhile, SA analysts lean bearish, suggesting Sell.

SA author Ron Barnett argues that Home Depot is an overpriced, mature company with limited growth potential. He points out that operating margins have returned to their pre-pandemic decline, while recent acquisitions raise concerns about capital discipline.

SA author Sanjay Chandiramani also downgraded the stock rating from Buy to Hold, citing growth assumptions and near-term headwinds. He notes that big-ticket purchases were down 6.8% in Q3, signaling macroeconomic challenges.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $3.03
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $39.01B
  • Earnings Insight: Home Depot has consistently outperformed EPS estimates in the past 8 quarters and revenue forecasts in 5 of those reports.

Also reporting: Lucid Group (NASDAQ:LCID), AMC Entertainment Holdings (NYSE:AMC), American Tower Corporation (NYSE:AMT), First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR), Lemonade (LMND), Li Auto (NASDAQ:LI), Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY), Intuit (INTU), Ocugen (OCGN), Hyliion Holdings (HYLN), Elanco Animal Health (ELAN), Axon Enterprise (AXON), Coupang (CPNG), Range Resources (RRC), B&G Foods (BGS) and Caesars Entertainment (CZR), and more.

Wednesday, February 26

NVIDIA

Headlining the week's earnings docket, chip giant Nvidia (NVDA) is set to release its results after the market closes on Wednesday. Analysts are expecting over 64% growth in year-over-year EPS growth and a nearly 73% surge in revenue.

Despite Wall Street’s Strong Buy consensus, Seeking Alpha analysts take a more cautious stance, assigning the stock a Hold rating, citing valuation concerns.

SA author Damir Tokic argues that Nvidia’s growth is slowing while its valuation remains in bubble territory. He believes the company could disappoint investors with its guidance, particularly if it acknowledges the DeepSeek effect on generative AI capital expenditures. If Nvidia signals a slowdown in AI capex, it could burst the Gen AI bubble, the analyst warns.

Conversely, Hataf Capital argues that Big Tech’s AI capex is exploding, with Amazon, Microsoft, Meta and Alphabet collectively spending over $300B, making Nvidia a clear winner. With $800B+ in AI capex commitments and a $2T+ total addressable market, Nvidia’s pricing power and next-gen Blackwell chips could drive earnings growth for years to come.

With Nvidia shares already up +100% over a period of one year, investors are keenly watching whether the AI-fueled momentum can continue or if cracks will start to show.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $0.85
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $38.13B
  • Earnings Insight: Nvidia outperformed revenue and EPS estimates all the last 8 quarters.

Also reporting: Salesforce (NYSE:CRM), Teladoc Health (NYSE:TDOC), Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW), Virgin Galactic Holdings (NYSE:SPCE), Lowe's Companies (NYSE:LOW), Beyond Meat (NASDAQ:BYND), Paramount Global (NASDAQ:PARA), MARA Holdings (NASDAQ:MARA), eBay (NASDAQ:EBAY), Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (NYSE:PBR), C3.ai (NYSE:AI), Expand Energy (EXE), EPR Properties (EPR), The TJX Companies (TJX), MannKind (MNKD), Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT), Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD), Agilent Technologies (A), Alcon (ALC), ACADIA Pharmaceuticals (ACAD), Ovintiv (OVV), Magnite (MGNI), Nutanix (NTNX), Geron Corporation (GERN), Stellantis N.V. (NYSE:STLA) and more.

Thursday, February 27

HP and Dell

HP (HPQ) and Dell (NYSE:DELL) are set to announce their quarterly earnings after the closing bell on Thursday, with analysts expecting mixed performance from both companies. The consensus on HP remains cautious, with both sell-side analysts and Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system assigning a Hold rating. Meanwhile, Dell receives a Buy recommendation from Wall Street analysts, though SA’s Quant Rating system remains cautious with a Hold.

In the broader PC market, Taiwan-based Acer, which competes with HP, Dell and Apple is considering shifting manufacturing out of China due to tariffs. Acer CEO Jason Chen stated that the company plans to raise PC prices by 10% in response to U.S. tariffs, according to The Telegraph.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: HPQ $0.74; DELL $2.51
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $13.35B; DELL $24.59B
  • Earnings Insight: HP has beaten EPS in 4 of the past 8 quarters, topping revenue estimates in 3 of those reports. Dell has exceeded revenue estimates in 6 of the past 8 quarters, while missed EPS expectations only once in that span.

Also reporting: Viatris (NASDAQ:VTRS), Medical Properties Trust (NYSE:MPW), Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN), HP (HPQ), Novavax (NVAX), Dell Technologies (DELL), Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH), Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD), APA Corporation (APA), Autodesk (ADSK), PENN Entertainment (PENN), Starwood Property Trust (STWD), Opendoor Technologies (OPEN), The Mosaic Company (MOS), The Macerich Company (MAC), Rocket Companies (RKT), OPKO Health (OPK), Sunrun (RUN), Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA), Clover Health Investments (CLOV), Bloom Energy (BE), The GEO Group (GEO), Hormel Foods (HRL), NetApp (NTAP) and more.

Friday, February 28

The week wraps up with a light earnings lineup, featuring pre-market reports from EOG Resources (NYSE:EOG), fuboTV (NYSE:FUBO), Fulgent Genetics (FLGT), and Frontline (FRO).

Also reporting: Butterfly Network (BFLY), McEwen Mining (MUX), BASF SE (OTCQX:BASFY), The AES Corporation (AES), Ginkgo Bioworks Holdings (DNA) and more.

data.title: Tariffs could add new cost pressures on already-tight power grid faced with surging demand
data.description:
U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said Friday that U.S. power plants will need to produce 10%-15% more electricity to meet growing energy needs to expand artificial intelligence across the U.S.

Burgum said the U.S. must avoid the example of Germany, which he said is trapped in a de-industrialization spiral because it moved too quickly away from fossil fuels.

American Electric Power (NASDAQ:AEP), Dominion Energy (NYSE:D), Duke Energy (DUK) and PPL Corp. (PPL) are among several utility companies that have unveiled plans in recent days to substantially raise their capital spending to accommodate expected higher demand for power.

But tariffs could make those plans much costlier, Jinjoo Lee wrote this week in the Wall Street Journal's "Heard On The Street" column, noting that transformers, the "nuts and bolts of the power system," look particularly vulnerable, already facing a shortage with demand expected to jump even more in the coming years.

The National Renewable Energy Laboratory estimates 55% of in-service distribution transformer units are older than 33 years and approaching their end of life, and distribution transformer capacity may need to increase 160%-260% by 2050 compared with 2021 levels to meet demand.

China, Mexico and Canada are important sources of electrical equipment to the U.S., with China accounting for 32% of U.S. low-voltage transformer equipment imports in 2024, Mexico for 36% of high-voltage transformer imports, and Canada for 16% of U.S. imports of high-voltage switchgear and 100% of imported utility poles, according to Wood Mackenzie.

Utilities typically undertake a lengthy process to test the reliability of transformers they are purchasing and tend to require custom specifications, so it is not an easy process to switch to a new supplier, Wood Mackenzie also notes.

If President Trump moves ahead with 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico, and imposes tariffs on copper as well, the consulting firm estimates transformer prices could increase by an additional 8%-9%.

"Building out America's AI dominance and reshoring manufacturing are popular policy objectives, but they might come at the cost of perhaps the most popular objective of all - lowering consumers' bills," Lee wrote.

ETFs: (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU), (RSPU)

data.title: Bill Ackman's biggest hits and misses
data.description:
Bill Ackman, founder and chief executive of Pershing Square Capital Management, has built his reputation as one of Wall Street's most high-profile corporate agitators on high-stakes activist campaigns that have reshaped companies and sparked intense debates.

Here's a look at some of his most prolific plays, both triumphs and misfires:

Canadian Pacific (NYSE:CP)

Ackman's campaign to overhaul railroad operator Canadian Pacific (CP) has been one of Pershing Square's biggest winners, netting the firm about $2.6 billion on its investment.

Pershing Square began buying into the railroad in September 2011. After securing control of the board, the firm installed Hunter Harrison as the CEO, leading to a dramatic turnaround.

However, Persing Square was forced to sell its stake in 2016 to pay back investors who were pulling out cash as the fund faced losses. Ackman labelled the sales as one of the firm's "greatest investment regrets."

Herbalife (NYSE:HLF)

In 2012, Ackman wagered $1 billion against Herbalife's (HLF) stock and publicly claimed the nutritional-supplements company was running a pyramid scheme where its distributors earned more from bringing in new recruits rather than selling its products.

This play pitched Ackman against rival billionaire Carl Icahn, who became Herbalife's (HLF) biggest shareholder after Ackman publicly disclosed his short position.

Ackman quietly exited his billion-dollar bet against Herbalife (HLF) in 2018 after the company's stock price continued to rise.

Chipotle (NYSE:CMG)

Ackman set his eyes on Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) following a food safety crisis at the fast casual chain that tarnished its reputation and sent its shares crashing.

Pershing Square invested roughly $1.2 billion in the company back in 2016, betting the Chipotle (CMG) would rebound. Ackman pushed for four new members to the company's board, and replaced CEO Steve Ells, who founded the chain, with former Taco Bell boss Brian Niccol.

According to Pershing Square's 13F filing, the firm owned 24.7 million shares in Chipotle (CMG) during the fourth quarter.

Target (NYSE:TGT)

Ackman's attempt to shake up discount retailer Target's (TGT) failed. In 2009, the company's shareholders rejected Pershing Square's slate of proposed directors, choosing instead to keep four incumbents on the retailer's board.

Pershing Square sold all its shares in the company in 2011. The firm reportedly lost $1.8 billion on its Target (TGT) investment.

data.title: Elon Musk asks all federal employees to explain their work or face dismissal
data.description:
Billionaire Elon Musk said on Saturday all federal employees will have to share what they've been working on in the last week or face dismissal.

"Consistent with President @realDonaldTrump’s instructions, all federal employees will shortly receive an email requesting to understand what they got done last week," Musk wrote in a post on X. "Failure to respond will be taken as a resignation."

Musk's statement seems to echo his moves at X, formerly known as Twitter. Just six months after closing his $44 billion acquisition of the microblogging platform, Musk claimed to have reduced the platform's headcount by 80% to 1500 employees.

Musk's Department of Government Efficiency has quickly moved through the federal government, cancelling contracts and firing thousands of workers across agencies

While DOGE claims the cost-cutting department has saved $55 billion in government spending so far, a Bloomberg report says the entity's official website only accounts for $16.6 billion of that.
